text: “Sooner would I go dry at Lammas-tide than be a man without a moon,” Christian went on, in that same broken chant. “’Tis said I am but the rames of a man, and fit for naught in my line at all; and I reckon that must be the reason o’t.”
source_text: “I’d sooner go without drink at Lammas-tide than be a man of no moon,” continued Christian, in the same shattered recitative. “’Tis said I be only the rames of a man, and no good for my race at all; and I suppose that’s the cause o’t.”

text:
Such was the false dawn of my lifetime,
Such the joys that once were mine:
But ere the noon, with tempests grim-time,
All my blossom’d bliss did pine.
Tho’ wavering fortune hath beguiled me—
She pled most fair, yet wrought but ill,
Of many a joy and hope despoiled me—
I bear a heart that shall sustain me still.
source_text:
Such was my life’s deceitful morning,
Such the pleasures I enjoyed:
But lang or noon, loud tempests storming
A’ my flowery bliss destroy’d.
Tho’ fickle fortune has deceiv’d me—
She promis’d fair, and perform’d but ill,
Of mony a joy and hope bereav’d me—
I bear a heart shall support me still.

text:
Who reared this little Alban house  
And drew the windows down so close  
My spirit may not see?  
Who’ll let me out some gala day,  
With engines fit to bear me away,  
Beyond all pomp and show?
source_text:
Who built this little Alban house
And shut the windows down so close
My spirit cannot see?
Who 'll let me out some gala day,
With implements to fly away,
Passing pomposity?

text:
O the magnet! the flesh again and again!
Go, dear friend, if need be renounce all else, and begin this day to
train thyself to courage, to reality, self-esteem, distinctness,
upliftedness,
Rest not until thou dost rivet and proclaim thyself from out thine own Personality.
source_text:
O the magnet! the flesh over and over!
Go, dear friend, if need be give up all else, and commence to-day to
inure yourself to pluck, reality, self-esteem, definiteness,
elevatedness,
Rest not till you rivet and publish yourself of your own Personality.

text:
‘Thou canst seek out and compass all that wit
May find or teach;—yet since thou wilt, come take
The lyre—be mine the glory of the gift—
Strike the sweet strings, and sing aloud, and wake
Thy glad delight out of many a fit
Of tranced sound—and with swift fingers make
Thy liquid-voiced companion speak with thee,—
It can speak measured music eloquently.
source_text:
‘Thou canst seek out and compass all that wit
Can find or teach;—yet since thou wilt, come take
The lyre—be mine the glory giving it—
Strike the sweet chords, and sing aloud, and wake
Thy joyous pleasure out of many a fit
Of tranced sound—and with fleet fingers make
Thy liquid-voiced comrade talk with thee,—
It can talk measured music eloquently.

text:
Yet, soul, be sure the first intent abides, and shall be wrought out,  
Perchance even now the hour is come.
source_text:
Yet soul be sure the first intent remains, and shall be carried out,
Perhaps even now the time has arrived.

text:
Alas for the silver, it holds such command,  
And alas for the love that is set upon land!  
A dowry is naught in a true lover’s parley,  
But give me my love, and a fig for the warld!
source_text:
O wae on the siller, it is sae prevailin’,
And wae on the love that is fixed on a mailen!
A tocher’s nae word in a true lover’s parle,
But gie me my love, and a fig for the warl’!

text:
In dewy glens thy burnie winds,
Where bonnie maids their linen dry,
Or hies by hazel shaws and braes,
Wi’ hawthorns high,
Where blackbirds blend with shepherds’ strains,
At close o’ day.
source_text:
In gowany glens thy burnie strays,
Where bonie lasses bleach their claes,
Or trots by hazelly shaws and braes,
Wi’ hawthorns gray,
Where blackbirds join the shepherd’s lays,
At close o’ day.

text:
By the moss-clad brink,
With me the Prince shall sit and think;
Shall muse in visioned Regency,
Enrapt in bright dreams of dawning Royalty.
source_text:
By the mossy brink,
With me the Prince shall sit and think;
Shall muse in visioned Regency,
Rapt in bright dreams of dawning Royalty.

text:
‘Genius hath beheld thee in her fervent dreams,
And shadowy presages of thy loveliness
Haunting the human heart, have there enwreathed
Those rooted hopes of some sweet bourne of bliss
Where friends and lovers meet to part no more.
Thou art the close of all desire and will,
The issue of all action; and the souls
That by the ways of an aspiring change
Have won thy haven of perpetual peace,
There rest from the eternity of toil
That wrought the fabric of thy perfection.
source_text:
‘Genius has seen thee in her passionate dreams,
And dim forebodings of thy loveliness
Haunting the human heart, have there entwined
Those rooted hopes of some sweet place of bliss
Where friends and lovers meet to part no more.
Thou art the end of all desire and will,
The product of all action; and the souls
That by the paths of an aspiring change
Have reached thy haven of perpetual peace,
There rest from the eternity of toil
That framed the fabric of thy perfectness.

text:
Heaven spare thee long to kiss the breath  
Of many flowery summers!  
And bless thy bonny lassies baith,  
I’m told they’re winsome kimmers!
source_text:
Heaven spare you lang to kiss the breath
O’ mony flow’ry simmers!
An’ bless your bonie lasses baith,
I’m tauld they’re loosome kimmers!

text:
In raptures sweet, this hour we meet,  
Wi’ fond affection an’ a’ that;  
But for how lang the flie may stang,  
Let inclination law that.  
For a’ that, &c.
source_text:
In raptures sweet, this hour we meet,
Wi’ mutual love an’ a’ that;
But for how lang the flie may stang,
Let inclination law that.
For a’ that, &c.

text:
Doubt me, my shadowed fellow!
Why, God might well be pleased
With but a shard of the love
Outpoured on thee unmeasured.
The whole of me, forever,—
What more can woman give?—
Speak swiftly, that I may endow thee
With the last delight I keep!
source_text:
Doubt me, my dim companion!
Why, God would be content
With but a fraction of the love
Poured thee without a stint.
The whole of me, forever,
What more the woman can, --
Say quick, that I may dower thee
With last delight I own!

text:
O unresistful, ever-restless race!  
O race beloved above all things! O my heart is sore with gentle love for all!  
O I grieve and yet I triumph, I am enrapt with love for all,  
Pioneers! O pioneers!
source_text:
O resistless restless race!
O beloved race in all! O my breast aches with tender love for all!
O I mourn and yet exult, I am rapt with love for all,
Pioneers! O pioneers!

text:
Shut not your doors against me, proud libraries,  
For what was wanting on all your well-fill’d shelves, yet  
most needful, I bring,  
Forth from the war emerging, a book have I made,  
The words of my book are nothing, the drift of it all,  
A book apart, not bound with the rest nor grasped by the intellect,  
But ye, untold latencies, shall thrill to every page.
source_text:
Shut not your doors to me proud libraries,
For that which was lacking on all your well-fill’d shelves, yet
needed most, I bring,
Forth from the war emerging, a book I have made,
The words of my book nothing, the drift of it every thing,
A book separate, not link’d with the rest nor felt by the intellect,
But you ye untold latencies will thrill to every page.

text:
I scanned my sentence soberly,  
Rehearsed it with mine eyes,  
To see that I had made no fault  
In its extremest clause, --
source_text:
I read my sentence steadily,
Reviewed it with my eyes,
To see that I made no mistake
In its extremest clause, --

text:
My love, she’s but a young lass yet,
My love, she’s but a young lass yet;

I rue the day I sought her, O!
I rue the day I sought her, O!

source_text:

text:
There was a solemn silence, and, gazing from the window,  
which stood unshuttered and unblinded, Timothy said:  
“Well, what a fess little bonfire that is, out by Cap’n Vye’s!  
’Tis burning just the same now as ever, upon my life.”
source_text: There was a solemn silence, and looking from the window, which was unshuttered and unblinded, Timothy said, “Well, what a fess little bonfire that one is, out by Cap’n Vye’s! ’Tis burning just the same now as ever, upon my life.”

text:
Forms, qualities, lives, humanity, speech, thoughts,  
The known ones, and the unknown, and those upon the stars,  
The stars themselves, some fashioned, others formless,  
Wonders like those countries, the soil, the trees, the cities, the dwellers,  
whatsoe'er they may be,  
Splendid suns, the moons and rings, the numberless mixtures and their workings,  
Such-like, and as fair as such-like, seen here or anywhere, lie  
set forth for a little space, which I stretch forth my arm and  
with my hand half compass,  
That holding the birth of each and all, the virtue, the seeds of all.
source_text:
Forms, qualities, lives, humanity, language, thoughts,
The ones known, and the ones unknown, the ones on the stars,
The stars themselves, some shaped, others unshaped,
Wonders as of those countries, the soil, trees, cities, inhabitants,
whatever they may be,
Splendid suns, the moons and rings, the countless combinations and effects,
Such-like, and as good as such-like, visible here or anywhere, stand
provided for a handful of space, which I extend my arm and
half enclose with my hand,
That containing the start of each and all, the virtue, the germs of all.

text:
“Shall I prove traitor to my knight, dost think?  
That were dishonor. I may not part from thee  
until, in knightly encounter upon the field,  
some overmatching champion shall fairly win  
and fairly wear me. I were to blame  
an I thought that this might ever hap.”
source_text: “Will I be traitor to my knight, dost think? That were dishonor. I may not part from thee until in knightly encounter in the field some overmatching champion shall fairly win and fairly wear me. I were to blame an I thought that that might ever hap.”

text:
“Well, ’tis so; yet a kindly Providence decreed it should be no worse,  
and I feel my thanks.  
As for a shepherd, there, I’m sure mis’ess ought to have made ye her baily—  
such a meet man for’t as you be.”
source_text: “Well, ’tis; but a happy Providence ordered that it should be no worse, and I feel my thanks. As to shepherd, there, I’m sure mis’ess ought to have made ye her baily—such a fitting man for’t as you be.”

text:
SPIRIT:
‘I was an infant when my mother went
To see an atheist burnt. She took me there:
The dark-robed priests were gathered round the pyre;
The multitude was gazing silently;
And as the culprit passed with dauntless mien,
A tempered scorn in his unfaltering eye,
Blent with a tranquil smile, shone calmly forth:
The eager fire crept round his manly limbs;
His steadfast eyes were soon with blindness seared;
His death-pang rent my heart! the senseless mob
Uttered a shout of triumph, and I wept.
“Weep not, my child!” cried my mother, “for that man
Has said, There is no God.”’
source_text:
SPIRIT:
‘I was an infant when my mother went
To see an atheist burned. She took me there:
The dark-robed priests were met around the pile;
The multitude was gazing silently;
And as the culprit passed with dauntless mien,
Tempered disdain in his unaltering eye,
Mixed with a quiet smile, shone calmly forth:
The thirsty fire crept round his manly limbs;
His resolute eyes were scorched to blindness soon;
His death-pang rent my heart! the insensate mob
Uttered a cry of triumph, and I wept.
“Weep not, child!” cried my mother, “for that man
Has said, There is no God.”’

text:
The passage to and from the magazine is now barred by the sentinels,  
They see so many unfamiliar faces they know not whom to trust.
source_text:
The transit to and from the magazine is now stopt by the sentinels,
They see so many strange faces they do not know whom to trust.

text:
The fable of heaven points to the soul,  
The soul is always lovely, it seems more or it seems less, it  
advances or it falls behind,  
It steps from its sheltered garden and gazes kindly on itself  
and gathers the world within it,  
Complete and pure the genitals once spurting, and complete and  
pure the womb adhering,  
The head full-grown, balanced and true, and the bowels and  
joints balanced and true.
source_text:
The myth of heaven indicates the soul,
The soul is always beautiful, it appears more or it appears less, it
comes or it lags behind,
It comes from its embower’d garden and looks pleasantly on itself
and encloses the world,
Perfect and clean the genitals previously jetting,and perfect and
clean the womb cohering,
The head well-grown proportion’d and plumb, and the bowels and
joints proportion’d and plumb.

text:
While plain-hearted neighbors  
Speak of the “early dead,”  
We, given to roundabout phrasing,  
Observe that birds have gone!
source_text:
While simple-hearted neighbors
Chat of the 'early dead,'
We, prone to periphrasis,
Remark that birds have fled!

text:
That Power which raised and still upholds  
This universal frame,  
Through countless ages, without a start,  
Was ever still the same.
source_text:
That Pow’r which rais’d and still upholds
This universal frame,
From countless, unbeginning time
Was ever still the same.

text:
A stock whose warrant shows in years and acts,  
What we are, we are—birth itself is answer enough to doubters,  
We bear ourselves as a blade is borne,  
We are mighty and immense within ourselves,  
We are ruling within ourselves, we are enough in the breadth of  
ourselves,  
We are fairest to ourselves and in ourselves,  
We stand self-poised in the center, sending out branches over the earth,  
From Missouri, Nebraska, or Kansas, turning ridicule to laughter.
source_text:
A breed whose proof is in time and deeds,
What we are we are, nativity is answer enough to objections,
We wield ourselves as a weapon is wielded,
We are powerful and tremendous in ourselves,
We are executive in ourselves, we are sufficient in the variety of
ourselves,
We are the most beautiful to ourselves and in ourselves,
We stand self-pois’d in the middle, branching thence over the world,
From Missouri, Nebraska, or Kansas, laughing attacks to scorn.

text:
We thirst at first; it is Nature’s law;
And later, when we die,
We plead for just a little water
From fingers passing by.
source_text:
We thirst at first, -- 't is Nature's act;
And later, when we die,
A little water supplicate
Of fingers going by.

text:
I know I have the finest of time and space, and was never gauged, and
never shall be gauged.
source_text:
I know I have the best of time and space, and was never measured and
never will be measured.

text:
She stung him, drained his steadfast charge,  
Yet, when her fiercest was complete,  
And he, unshaken, looked on her,  
She owned him for a man.
source_text:
She stung him, sapped his firm advance,
But, when her worst was done,
And he, unmoved, regarded her,
Acknowledged him a man.

text:
The hillsides must not know it,
Where I have roamed so far,
Nor whisper to the loving woods
The day that I depart,
source_text:
The hillsides must not know it,
Where I have rambled so,
Nor tell the loving forests
The day that I shall go,

text:
That heart, where mingled follies played,
Held firm and faithful still to Honour:
To show Clarinda’s dearest friend,
Was what a lover well might honor.
source_text:
That heart, where motley follies blend,
Was sternly still to Honour true:
To prove Clarinda’s fondest friend,
Was what a lover sure might do.

text:
They say that “time heals all,”  
Yet time has never healed;  
A real grief grows more sturdy,  
Like tendons, as years yield.
source_text:
They say that 'time assuages,' --
Time never did assuage;
An actual suffering strengthens,
As sinews do, with age.

text:
To limn an Angel’s ticklish wark,
Wi’ Nick, there’s little danger:
Ye’ll readily draw a lang-kenn’d face,
But no sae weel a stranger.—R. B.
source_text:

text:
And while I paused, it came to me that what he truly sang for was
not there alone,
Nor for his mate, nor for himself alone, nor for all the echoes thrown back,
But something delicate, secret, beyond and away,
A charge conveyed and hidden gift for those yet to be born.
source_text:
And while I paus’d it came to me that what he really sang for was
not there only,
Nor for his mate nor himself only, nor all sent back by the echoes,
But subtle, clandestine, away beyond,
A charge transmitted and gift occult for those being born.

text:
The Church lies in ruins, the State’s in disarray,
False hopes, harsh oppressions, and wars that waste away,
We daurna say it outright, but we ken wha’s to blame,—
There’ll never be peace till Jamie comes hame.
source_text:
The Church is in ruins, the State is in jars,
Delusions, oppressions, and murderous wars,
We dare na weel say’t, but we ken wha’s to blame,—
There’ll never be peace till Jamie comes hame.

text:
How Wisdom and Folly meet, mingle, and join,  
How Virtue and Vice blend their dark with their shine,  
How Genius, that glorious sire of invention,  
Unravels all order and ties up contention,  
I sing: if these mortals, the critics, should grumble,  
I care not, not I—let the Critics go mumble!
source_text:
How wisdom and Folly meet, mix, and unite,
How Virtue and Vice blend their black and their white,
How Genius, th’ illustrious father of fiction,
Confounds rule and law, reconciles contradiction,
I sing: If these mortals, the critics, should bustle,
I care not, not I—let the Critics go whistle!

text:
Beneath the teachings of things, spirits, Nature, governments,  
possessions, I swear I discern other teachings,  
Beneath it all, to me, is myself; to you, yourself, (the same  
dull, age-old song.)
source_text:
Underneath the lessons of things, spirits, Nature, governments,
ownerships, I swear I perceive other lessons,
Underneath all to me is myself, to you yourself, (the same
monotonous old song.)

text:
So—though without imagination—
A plain, keen apprehension
Of his mind’s labor had enlivened
The things it shaped; I think
It stirred some kind of thinking in the senses.
source_text:
Thus—though unimaginative—
An apprehension clear, intense,
Of his mind’s work, had made alive
The things it wrought on; I believe
Wakening a sort of thought in sense.

text:
Slip your hands into your pockets, Jonathan—you are a made man from
this day,
You are downright cute—and here is one of your bargains.
source_text:
Stick your hands in your pockets, Jonathan--you are a made man from
this day,
You are mighty cute--and here is one of your bargains.

text:
I never spoke to God,
Nor ever walked in heaven;
Yet I am sure of that one place
As though its map were given.
source_text:
I never spoke with God,
Nor visited in heaven;
Yet certain am I of the spot
As if the chart were given.

text:
Tell them wha hae the chief direction,
Scotland an’ I are in sore affliction,
Since first they set that cursed restriction
On aqua-vitae;
And stir them up to deep conviction,
And wake their pity.
source_text:
Tell them wha hae the chief direction,
Scotland an’ me’s in great affliction,
E’er sin’ they laid that curst restriction
On aqua-vitae;
An’ rouse them up to strong conviction,
An’ move their pity.

text:
Farewell to the mountains, snowmantled and high,
Farewell to the straths and the green vales lie nigh;
Farewell to the forests and wild-swaying woods,
Farewell to the torrents and rushing, loud floods.
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.
source_text:
Farewell to the mountains, high-cover’d with snow,
Farewell to the straths and green vallies below;
Farewell to the forests and wild-hanging woods,
Farewell to the torrents and loud-pouring floods.
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.

text:
Does spring conceal its gladness,
When buds and blossoms break?
Does the sower
Scatter seeds by night,
Or does the ploughman drive his share in dark?
source_text:
‘Does spring hide its joy,
When buds and blossoms grow?
Does the sower
Sow by night,
Or the ploughman in darkness plough?

text:
Oh, stop! must hatred and death come back?
Stop! must men still kill and die?
Stop! do not empty to the last dark drop
That urn of prophecy.
The world is weary with its ancient days,
Oh, might it sleep, or end, or rest always!
source_text:
Oh, cease! must hate and death return?
Cease! must men kill and die?
Cease! drain not to its dregs the urn
Of bitter prophecy.
The world is weary of the past,
Oh, might it die or rest at last!

text:
Nae mair by Babel’s streams we’ll weep,
To think on our Zion;
But hing our fiddles up to sleep,
Like baby-clouts a-dryin!
Come, set the pegs wi’ cheery cheep,
And o’er the thairms be tryin;
Oh, fine to see our elbows sweep,
And a’ like lamb-tails flyin
Fu’ fast the day.
source_text:
Nae mair by Babel’s streams we’ll weep,
To think upon our Zion;
And hing our fiddles up to sleep,
Like baby-clouts a-dryin!
Come, screw the pegs wi’ tunefu’ cheep,
And o’er the thairms be tryin;
Oh, rare to see our elbucks wheep,
And a’ like lamb-tails flyin
Fu’ fast this day.

“I’ll ask them once again.  
Whose are the beds, those little beds  
So close-set on the plain?”
source_text:

Whose are the beds, the tiny beds

text: Previous work has employed noising methods based on random n-gram modifications (Fevry and Phang, 2018); however, we instead depend on two straightforward, linguistically motivated noise functions. First, we train a bidirectional language model (BiLM; Peters et al., 2018) on the review corpus C. For each word in y, the BiLM produces a softmax distribution over the vocabulary, which can then be used to substitute words. Second, we use FLAIR 1 (Akbik et al., 2019), an off-the-shelf state-of-the-art syntactic chunker that exploits contextual embeddings, to shallow-parse each review r in corpus C. This yields a list of chunks C_r = {c_1, ..., c_K} together with the corresponding syntactic labels G_r = {g_1, ..., g_K} for each review r, which we use to replace and reorder chunks.
source_text: N }. Previous work has adopted noising techniques based on random n-gram alterations (Fevry and Phang, 2018) , however, we instead rely on two simple, linguistically informed noise functions. Firstly, we train a bidirectional language model (BiLM; Peters et al., 2018) on the review corpus C. For each word in y, the BiLM predicts a softmax word distribution which can be used to replace words. Secondly, we utilize FLAIR 1 (Akbik et al., 2019) , an off-theshelf state-of-the-art syntactic chunker that leverages contextual embeddings, to shallow parse each review r in corpus C. This results in a list of chunks C r = {c 1 , ..., c K } with corresponding syntactic labels G r = {g 1 , ..., g K } for each review r, which we use for replacing and rearranging chunks.

text: In this paper, we introduce a two-stage strategy to overcome these limitations, as shown in Figure 1. In the first stage, we employ an encoder-decoder model (SOW, for Source Order reWriting) to carry out transduction operations on different abstracted variants of the input sentence. These transductions produce candidate reorderings of words and constituents, which can then be combined to generate multiple plausible rearrangements of the input sentence. Each rearrangement defines the order in which the words of the source sentence should be visited; importantly, such orderings may encourage the model to passivize (i.e., visit the object before the subject), topicalize, or reorder clauses. We encode these orderings for our encoder-decoder paraphrase model (REAP, for REarrangement Aware Paraphrasing) using position embeddings, which are added to the source-sentence encoding to indicate the intended generation order (see Figure 2). This end-to-end procedure is motivated by the pre-ordering literature in machine translation (Xia and McCord, 2004; Collins et al., 2005); however, our setting explicitly requires considering a diverse set of possible orderings that correspond to distinct paraphrasing phenomena.
source_text: In this paper, we propose a two-stage approach to address these limitations, outlined in Figure 1 . First, we use an encoder-decoder model (SOW, for Source Order reWriting) to apply transduction operations over various abstracted versions of the input sentence. These transductions yield possible reorderings of the words and constituents, which can be combined to obtain multiple feasible rearrangements of the input sentence. Each rearrangement specifies an order that we should visit words of the source sentence; note that such orderings could encourage a model to passivize (visit the object before the subject), topicalize, or reorder clauses. These orderings are encoded for our encoder-decoder paraphrase model (REAP, for REarrangement Aware Paraphrasing) by way of po-sition embeddings, which are added to the source sentence encoding to specify the desired order of generation (see Figure 2 ). This overall workflow is inspired by the pre-ordering literature in machine translation (Xia and McCord, 2004; Collins et al., 2005) ; however, our setting explicitly requires entertaining a diverse set of possible orderings corresponding to different paraphrasing phenomena.

text: Relation extraction seeks to identify relations among entities in text and is an important component in many natural language processing applications. Early studies primarily aimed to predict relations between entities within a single sentence (Zeng et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2015a,b). Nevertheless, in real-world settings, important relational information between entities, such as biomedical findings, is often conveyed through multiple mentions that span sentence boundaries (Peng et al., 2017). Consequently, the extraction scope in the biomedical domain has recently been extended to the cross-sentence level (Gupta et al., 2018; Song et al., 2019). Figure 1 presents an example adapted from the DocRED dataset. The example contains four entities: Lutsenko, internal affairs, Yulia Tymoshenko, and Ukrainian. In this case, Lutsenko has two mentions, namely Lutsenko and He. Mentions referring to the same entity are marked with the same color. The solid and dotted lines denote intra-sentence and inter-sentence relations, respectively.
source_text: Relation extraction aims to detect relations among entities in the text and plays a significant role in a variety of natural language processing applications. Early research efforts focus on predicting relations between entities within the sentence (Zeng et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2015a,b ). However, valuable relational information between entities, such as biomedical findings, is expressed by multiple mentions across sentence boundaries in real-world scenarios (Peng et al., 2017) . Therefore, the scope Figure 1 : An example adapted from the DocRED dataset. The example has four entities: Lutsenko, internal affairs, Yulia Tymoshenko and Ukrainian. Here entity Lutsenko has two mentions: Lutsenko and He. Mentions corresponding to the same entity are highlighted with the same color. The solid and dotted lines represent intra-and inter-sentence relations, respectively. of extraction in biomedical domain has recently been expanded to cross-sentence level Gupta et al., 2018; Song et al., 2019) .

text: Although retrofitting brings pairs in the training dictionary closer together, its updates may also push apart translation pairs that lie outside the dictionary, since retrofitting ignores words beyond the training set. This can degrade both BLI test accuracy and downstream task performance. By contrast, projection-based methods tend to underfit, but they can identify translation pairs outside the training dictionary. To preserve the original CLWE’s correct translations, we retrofit both the training dictionary and a synthetic dictionary induced from the CLWE itself (orange, Figure 1). Prior work derives such dictionaries from CLWE via nearest-neighbor search (Mikolov et al., 2013). Instead, we employ cross-domain similarity local scaling (Conneau et al., 2018, CSLS), a translation heuristic that is more robust to hubs (Dinu et al., 2015), where a hub is a word that serves as the nearest neighbor of many words. We construct a synthetic dictionary D′ consisting of word pairs that are mutual CSLS nearest neighbors. We then retrofit the CLWE using the combined dictionary D ∪ D′. The synthetic dictionary preserves closely aligned word pairs in the original CLWE, which can sometimes improve downstream models.
source_text: While retrofitting brings pairs in the training dictionary closer, the updates may also separate translation pairs outside of the dictionary because retrofitting ignores words outside the training dictionary. This can hurt both BLI test accuracy and downstream task accuracy. In contrast, projectionbased methods underfit but can discover translation pairs outside the training dictionary. To keep the original CLWE's correct translations, we retrofit to both the training dictionary and a synthetic dictionary induced from CLWE (orange, Figure 1 ). Early work induces dictionaries from CLWE through nearest-neighbor search (Mikolov et al., 2013) . We instead use cross-domain similarity local scaling (Conneau et al., 2018, CSLS) , a translation heuristic more robust to hubs (Dinu et al., 2015 ) (a word is the nearest neighbor of many words). We build a synthetic dictionary D ′ with word pairs that are mutual CSLS nearest neighbors. We then retrofit the CLWE to a combined dictionary D ∪ D ′ . The synthetic dictionary keeps closely aligned word pairs in the original CLWE, which sometimes improves downstream models.

text: Recent studies have demonstrated the value of incorporating structural information during generation. By substituting the sequential encoder with a structure-aware graph encoder, such as a graph convolutional network (GCN) (Kipf and Welling, 2017) or graph-state LSTMs (Song et al., 2018), graph-to-sequence (Graph2Seq) methods can represent the structural properties of the input and therefore achieve better performance than Seq2Seq models on certain tasks. Nevertheless, these architectures widen the structural mismatch between the encoder and decoder. In other words, although the encoder processes the input as a graph, the decoder must generate the output text as a linear chain structure.
source_text: Recent research has shown the utility of incorporating structural information during generation. By replacing the sequential encoder with a structureaware graph encoder, such as a graph convolutional network (GCNs) (Kipf and Welling, 2017) or graph-state LSTMs (Song et al., 2018) , the resulting graph-to-sequence (Graph2Seq) methods can encode the structural information of the input and thus outperform Seq2Seq models on certain tasks. However, these architectures broaden the structural gap between the encoder and decoder. That is, while the encoder receives the input data as a graph, the decoder has to create the output text as a linear chain structure.

text: However, these approaches are either computationally demanding (Gal and Ghahramani, 2015) or require substantial effort to refine policy representations (Gai and Young, 2014). Furthermore, most existing studies of uncertainty in dialogue concentrate on the dialogue management component. End-to-end (E2E) dialogue retrieval models jointly encode a dialogue and a candidate response (Wu et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2018), under the assumption that the ground-truth response is always included in the candidate set, which is not the case in real-world deployment. Larson et al. (2019) recently demonstrated that classifiers that achieve strong performance on in-scope intent classification for task-oriented dialogue systems have difficulty detecting out-of-scope queries. The response selection task in the most recent Dialog System Technology Challenge (Chulaka Gunasekara and Lasecki, 2019) also explicitly states that “none of the proposed utterances is a good candidate” should be treated as a valid option.
source_text: However, these approaches are either computationally intensive (Gal and Ghahramani, 2015) or require significant work on refining policy representations (Gai and Young, 2014) . Moreover, most current uncertainty studies in dialog focus on the dialog management component. End-to-end (E2E) dialog retrieval models jointly encode a dialog and a candidate response (Wu et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2018) , assuming the ground truth is always present in the candidate set, which is not the case in production. Larson et al. (2019) recently showed that classifiers that perform well on in-scope intent classification for task-oriented dialog systems struggle to identify out-of-scope queries. The response selection task in the most recent Dialog System Technology Challenge (Chulaka Gunasekara and Lasecki, 2019) also explicitly mentions that "none of the proposed utterances is a good candidate" should be a valid option.

text: PPVAE is a model-agnostic framework, which implies that the encoders and decoders of PRE-TRAINVAE and PLUGINVAE can be adapted to operate under different settings. In this section, we describe the model architecture employed in our experiments. PRETRAINVAE. For the encoder, we adopt a one-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU) with 256 hidden units in each direction. Two linear Fully-Connected (FC) layers are used for the re-parameteristic trick. For the decoder, we use a Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) with 3 layers and 8 heads. In addition, we insert extra positional embeddings after each block, and the linearly transformed encoded vector is supplied as input to each block (Brock et al., 2019). For a fair comparison, we use the same encoder-decoder architecture for both S-VAE and CTRL-GEN. PLUGINVAE. The encoder is a two-layer FC network with 64/32 hidden units, taking input in d g dimensions and producing an additional linear output layer of d c units. The decoder is a two-layer FC network with 32/64 hidden units, which takes the latent variable in d c dimensions as input and outputs through a linear layer of d g units. The activation function used in the FC networks is LeakyReLU (Maas et al., 2013).
source_text: PPVAE is a model-agnostic approach, which means that both the encoders and encoders of PRE-TRAINVAE and PLUGINVAE can be modified to work under different settings. Here, we describe the model architecture used in our experiments. PRETRAINVAE. For the encoder, we use a onelayer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU) with 256 hidden units in each direction as its encoder. Two linear Fully-Connected (FC) layers are used for re-parameteristic trick . For the decoder, we use a Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) (3 layers, 8 heads). Additionally, we add extra positional embedding after each block, and the linearly transformed encoded vector is provided as input for each block (Brock et al., 2019) . For a fair comparison, we use the same encoder-decoder architecture for both S-VAE and CTRL-GEN. PLUGINVAE. The encoder is a two-layer FC network of 64/32 hidden units taking input in d g dimensions with an additional linear output layer of d c units. The decoder is a two-layer FC network of 32/64 hidden units taking the latent variable in d c dimensions as input with a linear output layer of d g units. The activation function used in the FC networks is LeakyRelu (Maas et al., 2013) .

text: In contrast, Natural Language Generation (NLG) is defined as the task of producing natural utterances from formal representations. Traditionally, this has been addressed through a pipelined framework (Reiter and Dale, 1997) involving content planning and surface realisation (Walker et al., 2001; Stent et al., 2004). More recently, NLG has been cast as an end-to-end learning problem in which text strings are generated by recurrent neural networks conditioned on the formal representation (Wen et al., 2015; Dušek and Jurcicek, 2016; Dušek et al., 2020; Balakrishnan et al., 2019; Tseng et al., 2019).
source_text: In comparison, Natural Language Generation (NLG) is scoped as the task of generating natural utterances from their formal representations. This is traditionally handled with a pipelined approach (Reiter and Dale, 1997) with content planning and surface realisation (Walker et al., 2001; Stent et al., 2004) . More recently, NLG has been formulated as an end-to-end learning problem where text strings are generated with recurrent neural networks conditioning on the formal representation (Wen et al., 2015; Dušek and Jurcicek, 2016; Dušek et al., 2020; Balakrishnan et al., 2019; Tseng et al., 2019) .

text: Sequence labeling systems are typically trained on clean text, although in real-world settings they often depend on an error-prone upstream component, such as Optical Character Recognition (OCR; Neudecker, 2016) or Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR; Parada et al., 2011). Sequence labeling is likewise frequently applied to user-generated text, which may include spelling errors or typos (Derczynski et al., 2013). Errors introduced in an upstream task are then propagated downstream, thereby reducing the performance of the end-to-end system (Alex and Burns, 2014). Although humans can readily cope with typos, misspellings, and even the complete omission of letters while reading (Rawlinson, Figure 1: An example of a labeling error on a slightly perturbed sentence. Our noise-aware methods correctly predicted the location (LOC) label for the first word, unlike the standard approach, which misclassified it as an organization (ORG). We also include a high-level illustration of our noise-aware training, in which the original sentence and its noisy variant are passed through the system together. The final loss is computed from both sets of features, which improves robustness to input perturbations.
source_text: Sequence labeling systems are generally trained on clean text, although in real-world scenarios, they often follow an error-prone upstream component, such as Optical Character Recognition (OCR; Neudecker, 2016) or Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR; Parada et al., 2011) . Sequence labeling is also often performed on user-generated text, which may contain spelling mistakes or typos (Derczynski et al., 2013) . Errors introduced in an upstream task are propagated downstream, diminishing the performance of the end-to-end system (Alex and Burns, 2014) . While humans can easily cope with typos, misspellings, and the complete omission of letters when reading (Rawlinson, Figure 1 : An example of a labeling error on a slightly perturbed sentence. Our noise-aware methods correctly predicted the location (LOC) label for the first word, as opposed to the standard approach, which misclassified it as an organization (ORG). We complement the example with a high-level idea of our noise-aware training, where the original sentence and its noisy variant are passed together through the system. The final loss is computed based on both sets of features, which improves robustness to the input perturbations.

text: Model compression and knowledge distillation: Earlier work in the vision community on large architectures such as AlexNet and ResNet has tackled this problem in two main ways. Studies on model compression apply quantization (Gong et al., 2014), low-precision training, and network pruning, as well as combinations of these methods (Han et al., 2016), to shrink the memory footprint. In contrast, knowledge distillation methods rely on student–teacher models. These methods include treating soft logits as supervision targets (Ba and Caruana, 2014), raising the softmax temperature to align it with the teacher’s output (Hinton et al., 2015), and using teacher representations (Romero et al., 2015) (see Cheng et al., 2017 for a survey), which imposes architectural constraints on the student, such as the embedding dimension. To mitigate this limitation, more recent work has combined task-specific distillation with pre-training the student model at an arbitrary embedding dimension, while still depending on transformer architectures (Turc et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). Izsak et al. (2019) and Shi et al. (2019) extend these ideas to sequence tagging for Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and Named Entity Recognition (NER) in English. The work most closely related to ours is Tsai et al. (2019), which extends the same line of research to multilingual NER.
source_text: Model compression and knowledge distillation: Prior works in the vision community dealing with huge architectures like AlexNet and ResNet have addressed this challenge in two ways. Works in model compression use quantization (Gong et al., 2014) , low-precision training and pruning the network, as well as their combination (Han et al., 2016) to reduce the memory footprint. On the other hand, works in knowledge distillation leverage student teacher models. These approaches include using soft logits as targets (Ba and Caruana, 2014) , increasing the temperature of the softmax to match that of the teacher (Hinton et al., 2015) as well as using teacher representations (Romero et al., 2015 ) (refer to (Cheng et al., 2017 ) for a survey). of the teacher models which adds architectural constraints (e.g., embedding dimension) on the student. In order to address this shortcoming, more recent works combine task-specific distillation with pre-training the student model with arbitrary embedding dimension but still relying on transformer architectures (Turc et al., 2019) ; (Jiao et al., 2019) ; (Zhao et al., 2019) . Izsak et al. (2019) ; Shi et al. (2019) extend these for sequence tagging for Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and Named Entity Recognition (NER) in English. The one closest to our work Tsai et al. (2019) extends the above for multilingual NER.

text: Tables 2 and 3 show several example dimensions induced by the model for both English and French. The factorization produced by the NMF model can be interpreted probabilistically (Gaussier and Goutte, 2005; Ding et al., 2008): the matrix W may be regarded as p(w|k), that is, the probability of a word given a latent dimension k. To constrain the network’s output to a particular topic, it would be straightforward to use p(w|k) as an additional prior probability distribution applied to each output. However, preliminary experiments showed that such a blind alteration of the output probability distribution for every word in the output sequence harms syntactic fluency. To reconcile syntactic fluency with topical consistency, we therefore make the weighting of the output probability distribution depend on its entropy: when the output distribution has low entropy, the neural network is confident in its choice of the next word in order to produce a well-formed sentence, and we do not modify it. By contrast, when the entropy is high, we adjust the distribution by using the topical distribution p(w|k) for a given latent dimension as a prior probability distribution—analogous to Equation 11—in order to inject the intended topic. The entropy threshold above which the modified distribution is applied is determined experimentally.
source_text: Tables 2 and 3 present a number of example dimensions induced by the model, for both English and French. The factorization that comes out of the NMF model can be interpreted probabilistically (Gaussier and Goutte, 2005; Ding et al., 2008) : matrix W can be considered as p(w|k), i.e. the probability of a word given a latent dimension k. In order to constrain the network's output to a certain topic, it would be straightforward to simply use p(w|k) as another prior probability distribution applied to each output. Initial experiments, however, indicated that such a blind modification of the output probability distribution for every word of the output sequence is detrimental to syntactic fluency. In order to combine syntactic fluency with topical consistency, we therefore condition the weighting of the output probability distribution on the entropy of that distribution: when the output distribution's entropy is low, the neural network is certain of its choice for the next word in order to generate a well-formed sentence, so we will not change it. On the other hand, when the entropy is high, we will modify the distribution by using the topical distribution p(w|k) for a particular latent dimension as prior probability distribution-analogous to Equation 11-in order to inject the desired topic. The entropy threshold, above which the modified distribution is used, is set experimentally.

text: (3) BERT-Pairwise uses the same model architecture as CMADE. For training, it constructs dialog pairs by randomly sampling dialog instances \(x_i\) and \(x_j\) and deriving \(z_{i,j}\) by comparing the corresponding self-reported user ratings \(y_i\) and \(y_j\). We exclude pairs for which \(y_i\) and \(y_j\) are equal. (4) BERT-Pairwise+Dev extends (3) by incorporating the 200 expert-annotated dialog pairs from the development set into the training data. We also evaluate CMADE variants that omit one or two of the three stages.
source_text: (3) BERT-Pairwise shares the same model architecture with CMADE. It constructs dialog pairs for training by randomly sample dialog pairs x i and x j and derive z i,j by comparing the corresponding self-reported user rating y i and y j . We discard the pairs with equal y i and y j . (4) BERT-Pairwise+Dev augments (3) by adding the 200 expert annotated dialog pairs in the development into the training data. We also compare the variants of CMADE which skips one or two of the three stages.

text: The approach of combining two perplexity estimates—one derived from a model trained on transcripts of speech produced by healthy controls and the other from a model trained on transcripts from patients with dementia—was introduced by Wankerl et al. (2017), who report an AUC of 0.83 using n-gram language models in a participant-level leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) evaluation on the DementiaBank dataset. Fritsch et al. (2019) subsequently enhanced the performance of this method by replacing the n-gram LM with a neural LM (an LSTM model), and report a higher AUC of 0.92. Nevertheless, it remains unclear whether this degree of accuracy reflects dementia-specific linguistic markers or instead results from markers of other substantial differences between the case and control groups, such as age (x = 71.4 vs. 63) and years of education (x = 12.1 vs. 14.3) (Becker et al., 1994).
source_text: The idea of combining two perplexity estimates -one from a model trained on transcripts of speech produced by healthy controls and the other from a model trained on transcripts from patients with dementia -was developed by Wankerl et al. (2017) who report an AUC of 0.83 using n-gram LMs in a participant-level leave-one-out-crossvalidation (LOOCV) evaluation across the DementiaBank dataset. Fritsch et al. (2019) further improved performance of this approach by substituting a neural LM (a LSTM model) for the n-gram LM, and report an improved AUC of 0.92. However, it is currently unclear as to whether this level of accuracy is due to dementia-specific linguistic markers, or a result of markers of other significant differences between the case and control group such as age (x = 71.4 vs. 63) and years of education (x= 12.1 vs. 14.3) (Becker et al., 1994).

text: Knowledge Graph Embedding (KGE) model. A KGE model (Nickel et al., 2016) assigns an embedding to every entity and every relation. These embeddings are dense vector representations learned under a link prediction objective. They are then used to calculate a KGE model-specific score s(i, k, j) for a triple (i, k, j); the objective is to assign high scores to true triples and low scores to incorrect triples.
source_text: Knowledge Graph Embedding (KGE) model. A KGE model (Nickel et al., 2016) associates an embedding with each entity and each relation. The embeddings are dense vector representations that are learned with an LP objective. They are used to compute a KGE model-specific score s(i, k, j) for a triple (i, k, j); the goal is to predict high scores for true triples and low scores for wrong triples.

source_text: While structured prediction is traditionally presented at the output layer, recent applications have deployed structured models broadly within neural networks (Johnson et al., 2016; Kim et al., 2017; Yogatama et al., 2016, inter alia) . Torch-Struct aims to encourage this general use case. To illustrate, we consider a latent tree model. ListOps (Nangia and Bowman, 2018 ) is a dataset of mathematical functions. Each input/output pair consists of a prefix expression x and its result y, e.g.

text: Unsupervised speech representation learning, which seeks to learn a mapping from surface-level inputs, such as audio waveforms or spectrograms, to higher-level representations using only unlabeled speech, has attracted considerable attention in recent years (Baevski et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020; Song et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2019; Schneider et al., 2019; Chorowski et al., 2019; Pascual et al., 2019; Oord et al., 2018; Kamper, 2019; Chen et al., 2018; Milde and Biemann, 2018; Chung et al., 2016; Hsu et al., 2017). A substantial number of these methods rely on self-supervised learning, in which the training target is derived from the input itself, thereby enabling model training in a supervised fashion. Chung et al. (2019) introduce a technique termed Autoregressive Predictive Coding (APC), which trains an RNN to predict a future frame that lies n steps ahead of the current position, conditioned on a context such as preceding frames. The corresponding target can be generated straightforwardly by right-shifting the input by n steps. Their underlying idea is that the model must learn an effective summary of the past and encode this information in its hidden states in order to satisfy the objective. Once training is complete, the hidden states of the RNN are used as the learned representations, and they have been shown to capture speech-related information, including phonetic and speaker characteristics, that is beneficial for a range of speech tasks (Chung and Glass, 2020).
source_text: Unsupervised speech representation learning, which aims to learn a function that transforms surface features, such as audio waveforms or spectrograms, to higher-level representations using only unlabeled speech, has received great attention recently (Baevski et al., , 2020 Liu et al., 2020; Song et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2019; Schneider et al., 2019; Chorowski et al., 2019; Pascual et al., 2019; Oord et al., 2018; Kamper, 2019; Chen et al., 2018; Milde and Biemann, 2018; Chung et al., 2016; Hsu et al., 2017) . A large portion of these approaches leverage self-supervised training, where the learning target is generated from the input itself, and thus can train a model in a supervised manner. Chung et al. (2019) propose a method called Autoregressive Predictive Coding (APC), which trains an RNN to predict a future frame that is n steps ahead of the current position given a context such as the past frames. The training target can be easily generated by right-shifting the input by n steps. Their intuition is that the model is required to produce a good summarization of the past and encode such knowledge in the hidden states so as to accomplish the objective. After training, the RNN hidden states are taken as the learned representations, and are shown to contain speech information such as phonetic and speaker content that are useful in a variety of speech tasks (Chung and Glass, 2020) .

For instance, T B-loc,B-team corresponds to p(B-loc | B-team). However, in the few-shot setting, the model encounters different label sets in the source domains (train) and the target domains (test). source_text: Conventionally, such probabilities are learned from training data and stored in a transition matrix T N ×N , where N is the number of labels. For example, T B-loc,B-team corresponds to p(B-loc | B-team). But in the few-shot setting, a model faces different label sets in the source domains (train) and the target domains (test). This mismatch on labels blocks the trained transition scorer directly working on a target domain.

text: Natural language interfaces (NLIs) have long been regarded as the “holy grail” of natural language understanding and human-computer interaction (Woods et al., 1972; Codd, 1974; Hendrix et al., 1978; Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005). Nevertheless, early efforts to develop NLIs for databases failed to attain the anticipated level of success, largely because of limitations in language understanding capability, among other factors (Androutsopoulos et al., 1995; Jones and Galliers, 1995). Recently, NLIs have attracted growing attention, driven by interest in building virtual assistants, dialogue systems, and semantic parsing systems. Within this trend, NLIs for databases have been at the forefront, with multiple studies concentrating on translating natural language utterances into executable SQL queries (Text-to-SQL parsing).
source_text: Natural language interfaces (NLIs) have been the "holy grail" of natural language understating and human-computer interaction for decades (Woods et al., 1972; Codd, 1974; Hendrix et al., 1978; Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005) . However, early attempts in building NLIs to databases did not achieve the expected success due to limitations in language understanding capability, among other reasons (Androutsopoulos et al., 1995; Jones and Galliers, 1995) . NLIs have been receiving increasing attention recently motivated by interest in developing virtual assistants, dialogue systems, and Figure 1 : An example of human interaction with a Textto-SQL system to correct the interpretation of an input utterance. The system generates an initial SQL parse, explains it in natural language, and displays the execution result. Then, the system uses the human-provided natural language feedback to correct the initial parse. semantic parsing systems. NLIs to databases were at the forefront of this wave with several studies focusing on parsing natural language utterances into an executable SQL queries (Text-to-SQL parsing).

text: For RL-based text generation, the majority of existing studies adopt a model-free framework, which has been criticized for its high variance and low sample efficiency (Sutton and Barto, 1998). By contrast, although model-based RL methods do not exhibit these drawbacks, they are generally difficult to train in complex environments. Moreover, the capacity of the environment model typically constrains the learned policy. Recent advances in model-based RL (Gu et al., 2016; Kurutach et al., 2018; Nagabandi et al., 2017) integrate the strengths of both paradigms and have yielded better performance by learning a model-free policy with the aid of an environment model. In addition, model-based RL has recently been applied to tasks with extremely sparse rewards through curiosity-driven methods (Pathak et al., 2017).
source_text: For RL-based text generation, most existing works rely on a model-free framework, which has been criticized for its high variance and poor sample efficiency (Sutton and Barto, 1998) . On the other hand, while model-based RL methods do not suffer from these issues, they are usually difficult to train in complex environments. Further, a learned policy is usually restricted by the capacity of an environment model. Recent developments on model-based RL (Gu et al., 2016; Kurutach et al., 2018; Nagabandi et al., 2017) combine the advantages of these two approaches, and have achieved improved performance by learning a model-free policy, assisted by an environment model. In addition, model-based RL has been employed recently to solve problems with extremely sparse rewards, with curiosity-driven methods (Pathak et al., 2017) .

text: Identifying BABY-STEPs Our learning framework requires an agent to execute microinstructions (i.e., BABY-STEPs). However, existing datasets (Anderson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019) do not offer fine-grained segmentations of lengthy instructions. We therefore adopt a template-matching strategy to combine adjacent sentences into BABY-STEPs. First, we extract the noun phrase via POS tagging. We then apply heuristic rules to divide a long instruction into shorter segments based on punctuation and landmark phrases (i.e., words denoting concrete objects). We provide the full details in the Appendix.
source_text: Identifying BABY-STEPs Our learning approach requires an agent to follow microinstructions (i.e., the BABY-STEPs). Existing datasets (Anderson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019) do not provide fine-grained segmentations of long instructions. Therefore, we use a template matching approach to aggregate consecutive sentences into BABY-STEPs. First, we extract the noun phrase using POS tagging. Then, we employs heuristic rules to chunk a long instruction into shorter segments according to punctuation and landmark phrase (i.e., words for concrete objects). We document the details in the Appendix.

text: In recent years, recurrent neural network (RNN)-based architectures (Chopra et al., 2016; Gu et al., 2016; Nallapati et al., , 2017 See et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018b,a; Zhu et al., 2019) have achieved state-of-the-art performance in text summarization. text: These metrics, however, are surface-oriented and, by construction, cannot capture semantic equivalence when there is little or no lexical overlap. To address these shortcomings, some studies have leveraged static word embeddings (Mikolov et al., 2013b) and trained supervised embedding-based metrics on sufficiently large datasets for which human judgments of translation quality are available (Shimanaka et al., 2018). With the advent of contextual word embeddings (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019), a number of semantic metrics that also take word order into account have been proposed. For instance, Clark et al. (2019) present a semantic metric based on sentence mover’s similarity and contextualized ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018). Likewise, Zhang et al. (2019) propose a reference-based semantic similarity metric grounded in contextualized BERT representations (Devlin et al., 2019). Zhao et al. (2019) extend this line of research with MoverScore, which computes mover’s distance, namely the optimal soft alignment between the tokens of two sentences, using similarities between their contextualized embeddings. Mathur et al. (2019) train a supervised BERT-based regressor for reference-based MT evaluation.
source_text: These metrics, however, operate at the surface level, and by design fail to recognize semantic equivalence lacking lexical overlap. To overcome these limitations, some research efforts exploited static word embeddings (Mikolov et al., 2013b) and trained embedding-based supervised metrics on sufficiently large datasets with available human judgments of translation quality (Shimanaka et al., 2018) . With the development of contextual word embeddings (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019) , we have witnessed proposals of semantic metrics that account for word order. For example, Clark et al. (2019) introduce a semantic metric relying on sentence mover's similarity and the contextualized ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018) . Similarly, Zhang et al. (2019) describe a reference-based semantic similarity metric based on contextualized BERT representations (Devlin et al., 2019) . Zhao et al. (2019) generalize this line of work with their MoverScore metric, which computes the mover's distance, i.e., the optimal soft alignment between tokens of the two sentences, based on the similarities between their contextualized embeddings. Mathur et al. (2019) train a supervised BERT-based regressor for reference-based MT evaluation.

text: Human Evaluation In addition to automatic metrics, we conduct human evaluation on two tasks to assess the capabilities of our All Tasks MT conversational agent: the knowledge-grounding task Wizard of Wikipedia (WoW) and the image-grounding task Image Chat. We adopt the same evaluation protocols as in ; Shuster et al. (2018) , comparing our approach against the existing methods listed in Table 7 . This requires collecting 100 human-bot conversations for WoW via crowdworkers, with 8–10 turns each, spanning both seen topics (those present in the training set) and unseen topics, as well as 500 image-based responses for Image Chat. A separate group of crowdworkers then evaluates the models in pairwise comparisons according to the ACUTE-Eval procedure of (Li et al., 2019b) , where they are asked to select which is “the more engaging response” for Image Chat (1500 trials) and “Who would you prefer to talk to for a long conversation?” for WoW (400 trials).
source_text: Human Evaluation In addition to automatic metrics, we perform human evaluation on two of the tasks to assess the abilities of our All Tasks MT conversational agent: the knowledge grounding task Wizard of Wikipedia (WoW) and the image grounding task Image Chat. We follow the same evaluation protocols as in ; Shuster et al. (2018) , comparing our method to the existing approaches referenced in Table 7 . This involves collecting 100 human-bot conversations for WoW using crowdworkers, involving 8-10 turns each, across seen topics (seen in the training set) and unseen topics, and 500 image-based responses for Image Chat. A separate set of crowdworkers are then used to compare models pairwise following the ACUTE-Eval procedure of (Li et al., 2019b) , where they are asked to choose which is "the more engaging response" for Image Chat (1500 trials) and "Who would you prefer to talk to for a long conversation?" for WoW (400 trials).

text: Paraphrase Generation In contrast to earlier seq2seq methods for paraphrasing (Hasan et al., 2016; Gupta et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018), our model can achieve substantially greater controllability through an interpretable control mechanism. As with these approaches, we can draw on a broad range of resources for training, including backtranslation (Pavlick et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2019) as well as other curated data sources (Fader et al., 2013; Lan et al., 2017).
source_text: Paraphrase Generation Compared to prior seq2seq approaches for paraphrasing (Hasan et al., 2016; Gupta et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018) , our model is able to achieve much stronger controllability with an interpretable control mechanism. Like these approaches, we can leverage a wide variety of resources to train on, including backtranslation (Pavlick et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2019) or other curated data sources (Fader et al., 2013; Lan et al., 2017) .

text: Relation-oriented Tasks (Table 2b) - Relation Extraction (Hendrickx et al., 2010) RE focuses on the relation holding between two entities. - Coreference (Pradhan et al., 2012) Coreference resolution seeks to connect named, nominal, and pronominal mentions that denote the same concept, whether within a single sentence or across sentences. - Semantic Role Labeling (Gildea and Jurafsky, 2002) SRL is intended to identify the arguments of a predicate (verb or noun) and assign them semantic roles with respect to that predicate. - Open Information Extraction (Banko et al., 2007; Niklaus et al., 2018) Unlike the fixed relation labels used in RE, OpenIE aims to extract open-domain predicates and their arguments (typically subjects and objects) from a sentence. - Dependency Parsing (Kübler et al., 2009) Here, spans consist of single-word tokens, and a relation connects a word to its syntactic head with the appropriate dependency label. - Opinion Role Labeling (Yang and Cardie, 2013) ORL identifies spans that express opinions, together with the holders and targets associated with those opinions.
source_text: • Relation-oriented Tasks (Table 2b) -Relation Extraction (Hendrickx et al., 2010) RE concerns the relation between two entities. -Coreference (Pradhan et al., 2012) Coreference resolution is to link named, nominal, and pronominal mentions that refer to the same concept, within or beyond a single sentence. -Semantic Role Labeling (Gildea and Jurafsky, 2002 ) SRL aims to identify arguments of a predicate (verb or noun) and classify them with semantic roles in relation to the predicate. -Open Information Extraction (Banko et al., 2007; Niklaus et al., 2018) In contrast to the fixed relation types in RE, OpenIE aims to extract open-domain predicates and their arguments (usually subjects and objects) from a sentence. -Dependency Parsing (Kübler et al., 2009) Spans are single-word tokens and a relation links a word to its syntactic parent with the corresponding dependency type. -Opinion Role Labeling (Yang and Cardie, 2013) ORL detects spans that are opinion expressions, as well as holders and targets related to these opinions.

text: But how can we quantitatively assess the quality of the generated QA pairs? Common evaluation measures for text generation, such as BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), ROUGE (Lin and Hovy, 2002), and METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), indicate only how similar the generated QA pairs are to the Ground-Truth (GT) QA pairs, and they do not directly reflect their true quality (Nema and Khapra, 2018; Zhang and Bansal, 2019). Accordingly, we adopt the QA-based Evaluation (QAE) metric introduced by Zhang and Bansal (2019), which assesses how closely the generated QA pairs conform to the distribution of GT QA pairs. However, in a semi-supervised learning scenario in which GT labels are already available, we require novel QA pairs that differ from the GT QA pairs for the extra QA pairs to be genuinely useful. We therefore introduce a new metric, Reverse QAE (R-QAE), which yields low values when the generated QA pairs are novel and diverse.
source_text: But how should we quantitatively measure the quality of the generated QA pairs? Popular evaluation metrics (e.g. BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) , ROUGE (Lin and Hovy, 2002) , METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005)) for text generation only tell how similar the generated QA pairs are to Ground-Truth (GT) QA pairs, and are not directly correlated with their actual quality (Nema and Khapra, 2018; Zhang and Bansal, 2019) . Therefore, we use the QA-based Evaluation (QAE) metric proposed by Zhang and Bansal (2019) , which measures how well the generated QA pairs match the distribution of GT QA pairs. Yet, in a semi-supervised learning setting where we already have GT labels, we need novel QA pairs that are different from GT QA pairs for the additional QA pairs to be truly effective. Thus, we propose a novel metric, Reverse QAE (R-QAE), which is low if the generated QA pairs are novel and diverse.

text: Our work is connected to several established research directions, including semantic parsing, learning through interaction (Li et al., 2017a; Hancock et al., 2019; Li et al., 2017b, inter alia), and learning from natural language supervision (Srivastava et al., 2017; Co-Reyes et al., 2019; Srivastava et al., 2018; Hancock et al., 2018; Ling and Fidler, 2017, inter alia). We next discuss our relationships to the most relevant prior work.

text: In this paper, we investigate German number inflection, which has been recognized as a critical test case for connectionist modeling (Köpcke, 1988; Bybee, 1995; Marcus et al., 1995; Clahsen, 1999b). The German plural system contains eight plural markers (cf. Table 1), none of which constitutes a numerical majority in either type or token frequency. Distinct linguistic contexts favor different plural markers (e.g. Köpcke, 1988; Wiese, 1996; Yang, 2016), and even the well-known rare suffix /-s/ remains productive, in the sense that speakers readily extend it to novel words. 1 In their analysis of the German plural system, Marcus et al. (1995, henceforth M95) maintain that neural networks generalize the most frequent patterns to unfamiliar inputs and therefore have difficulty representing productive yet infrequent classes such as /-s/. We test this claim using the novel Germanlike nouns developed by M95.
source_text: In this paper, we examine German number inflection, which has been identified as a crucial test case for connectionist modeling (Köpcke, 1988; Bybee, 1995; Marcus et al., 1995; Clahsen, 1999b) . The German plural system features eight plural markers (c.f. Table 1) , none of which hold a numerical majority in type or token frequency. Different linguistic environments favor different plural markers (e.g. Köpcke, 1988; Wiese, 1996; Yang, 2016) , and even the famously rare suffix /-s/ is nonetheless productive, in the sense that speakers readily extend it to new words. 1 In their analysis of the German plural system, Marcus et al. (1995, henceforth M95) argue that neural networks generalize the most frequent patterns to unfamiliar inputs, and thus struggle to represent productive but rare classes such as /-s/. We investigate that claim using the novel Germanlike nouns M95 developed.

(2019), discussed above, preliminary cascades based on phone-like units have been investigated for low-resource speech translation, driven by the translation of unwritten languages, for which a conventional cascade would not be feasible. In this direction, Bansal et al. (2018) employed unsupervised term discovery, and Wilkinson et al. (2016) generated synthetic speech; however, these methods were evaluated only with respect to precision and recall, and were not assessed under both higher-resource and natural speech data conditions.
