text: The Ubuntu Dialogue Corpus comprises 1 million dialogue turns drawn from Ubuntu chat logs and centered on technical support related to Ubuntu (Lowe et al., 2015). The Twitter Triple Corpus is a collection of 4K dialogue triples extracted from Twitter. OpenSubtitles is an extensive subtitle repository covering multiple genres, although the lack of speaker turn annotations makes it challenging to convert into dialogue format (Lison and Tiedemann, 2016). Ameixa et al. (2013) apply heuristics to transform OpenSubtitles into dialogues, achieving only limited success. Clark and Schaefer (1989) demonstrate grounding in conversation through examples from the London-Lund Corpus (Greenbaum and Svartvik, 1990), a corpus of complete conversations annotated with prosodic and paralinguistic features. A second version of the corpus was later compiled using the same annotation standards as the first, but drawing on more recent spoken and written data (Põldvere et al., 2017).
source_text: The Ubuntu Dialogue Corpus contains 1 million dialogue turns extracted from Ubuntu chat logs, which discuss Ubuntu-related technical support (Lowe et al., 2015) . The Twitter Triple Corpus is a dataset of 4K dialogue triples extracted from Twitter . OpenSubtitles is a huge collection of subtitles that span various genres, but the absence of speaker turn annotations make it difficult to modify into dialogue format (Lison and Tiedemann, 2016) . Ameixa et al. (2013) use heuristics to reformat OpenSubtitles into dialogues with some limited success. Clark and Schaefer (1989) illustrate grounding in conversations with examples from the London-Lund Corpus (Greenbaum and Svartvik, 1990) , a corpus of full conversations annotated with prosodic and paralinguistic features. A second version of the corpus was compiled with the same annotations standards as the first using more recent spoken and text data (Põldvere et al., 2017) .

text: Other approaches resemble ours insofar as they exploit the structure supplied by interview prompts. Al Hanai et al. (2018) and Gong and Poellabauer (2017) introduce models that derive distinct feature sets for responses to each prompt type present in their corpus. Yet these methods depend on manually determining the set of unique prompts. In contrast, our model can automatically learn a new, task-specific prompt categorization. Lubis et al. (2018) apply K-means clustering to prompts in order to assign them to latent dialogue-act categories, which are then used as features in a neural dialogue system. Our method extends this idea of introducing a separate unsupervised clustering stage by letting the learning objective guide the clustering process. Our approach is also related to that of Chaturvedi et al. (2014) in that it automatically classifies different segments of the conversation. However, they rely on domain-specific handcrafted features and discrete latent variables for this classification. By contrast, our approach can exploit the neural architecture to automatically discover features that are useful for this categorization.
source_text: Other approaches are similar to ours in the sense that they utilize the structure provided by interview prompts. Al Hanai et al. (2018) and Gong and Poellabauer (2017) propose models that extract separate sets of features for responses to each unique prompt in their corpus. However, these approaches require manually identifying unique prompts. Our model can instead automatically learn new, task-specific categorization of prompts. Lubis et al. (2018) perform a K-means clustering of prompt to assign prompts to latent dialogue act categories. These are used as features in a neural dialogue system. Our approach expands upon this idea of incorporating a separate unsupervised clustering step by allowing the learning goal to influence the clustering. Our approach is also related to that of Chaturvedi et al. (2014) in that it automatically categorizes various parts of the conversation. However, they use domain-specific handcrafted features and discrete latent variables for this categorization. Our approach instead can leverage the neural architecture to automatically identify features useful for this categorization.

text: The NLP community has experienced a major shift with the advent of pre-trained self-supervised models. These models typically contain hundreds of millions of parameters (Peters et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019). Among them, BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) achieves substantial gains in accuracy. However, as one of the largest models ever proposed in NLP, BERT is hindered by its large model size and high latency, which makes it impractical to deploy BERT’s capabilities on resource-constrained mobile devices for mobile-based machine translation, dialogue modeling, and similar applications.
source_text: The NLP community has witnessed a revolution of pre-training self-supervised models. These models usually have hundreds of millions of parameters (Peters et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019) . Among these models, BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) shows substantial accuracy improvements. However, as one of the largest models ever in NLP, BERT suffers from the heavy model size and high latency, making it impractical for resource-limited mobile devices to deploy the power of BERT in mobile-based machine translation, dialogue modeling, and the like.

text: 6. Citeomatic The strongest baseline is Citeomatic, an academic-paper representation model trained on the citation graph using sampled triplets. Citeomatic produces representations that are L2-normalized weighted sums of title and abstract embeddings, which are learned from the citation graph with dynamic negative sampling. Citeomatic embeddings are 75-dimensional. 7. SGC Because our algorithm is trained on citation-graph data, we also compare against a state-of-the-art graph representation learning model: SGC (Simple Graph Convolution) (Wu et al., 2019a), a graph convolution network. Another possible comparator would have been Graph-SAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017b), but SGC with no learning outperformed an unsupervised GraphSAGE variant on the Reddit dataset 16. Note that SGC with no learning reduces to graph propagation over node features (in our case, the nodes are academic documents). Following Hamilton et al. (2017a), we used SIF features as node representations and ran SGC with a range of parameter k, where k is the number of times the normalized adjacency is multiplied by the SIF feature matrix. We varied k from 1 to 8 inclusive and selected it using a validation set. For the node features, we chose the SIF model with a = 0.0001, since this model was observed to perform well. This baseline is also 300-dimensional.
source_text: 6. Citeomatic The most relevant baseline is Citeomatic , which is an academic paper representation model that is trained on the citation graph via sampled triplets. Citeomatic representations are an L2 normalized weighted sum of title and abstract embeddings, which are trained on the citation graph with dynamic negative sampling. Citeomatic embeddings are 75-dimensional. 7. SGC Since our algorithm is trained on data from the citation graph, we also compare to a state-ofthe-art graph representation learning model: SGC (Simple Graph Convolution) (Wu et al., 2019a) , which is a graph convolution network. An alternative comparison would have been Graph-SAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017b) , but SGC (with no learning) outperformed an unsupervised variant of GraphSAGE on the Reddit dataset 16 , Note that SGC with no learning boils down to graph propagation on node features (in our case nodes are academic documents). Following Hamilton et al. (2017a), we used SIF features as node representations, and applied SGC with a range of parameter k, which is the number of times the normalized adjacency is multiplied by the SIF feature matrix. Our range of k was 1 through 8 (inclusive), and was chosen with a validation set. For the node features, we chose the SIF model with a = 0.0001, as this model was observed to be a high-performing one. This baseline is also 300 dimensional.

text: We follow Chung et al. (2019) and train APC on the audio component of the LibriSpeech (Panayotov et al., 2015) train-clean-360 subset, which comprises 360 hours of read speech from speakers. The input features are 80-dimensional log Mel spectrograms, that is, \(x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{80}\). Both RNN and RNN aux consist of a 3-layer, 512-dimensional unidirectional GRU (Cho et al., 2014) with residual connections across two successive layers. Accordingly, \(W, W_{\mathrm{aux}} \in \mathbb{R}^{512 \times 80}\). We set \(\lambda = 0.1\) and the sampling probability \(P = 0.15\), meaning that each frame has a 15% chance of being chosen as an anchor position. The values of \(P\) and \(\lambda\) are determined according to the validation loss of \(L_f\) on a small data split. All models are trained for 100 epochs with Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015), using a batch size of 32 and a learning rate of \(10^{-3}\).
source_text: We follow Chung et al. (2019) and use the audio portion of the LibriSpeech (Panayotov et al., 2015) train-clean-360 subset, which contains 360 hours of read speech produced by speakers, for training APC. The input features are 80dimensional log Mel spectrograms, i.e., x t ∈ R 80 . Both RNN and RNN aux are a 3-layer, 512-dim unidirectional GRU (Cho et al., 2014) network with residual connections between two consecutive layers . Therefore, W, W aux ∈ R 512×80 . λ is set to 0.1 and the sampling probability P is set to 0.15, that is, each frame has a 15% of chance to be selected as an anchor position. P and λ are selected based on the validation loss of L f on a small data split. All models are trained for 100 epochs using Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015) with a batch size of 32 and a learning rate of 10 −3 .

text: Teacher hidden representations and distillation schedule: Although internal teacher representations can facilitate distillation, a naive combination degrades the student model. We demonstrate that a distillation schedule based on stagewise optimization and gradual unfreezing with a cosine learning-rate scheduler (D4.1 + D4.2 in Table 3) achieves the best performance. We also find that the teacher’s middle layers are more readily distilled by shallow students and yield the strongest results (Table 8). In addition, student performance improves when using larger and stronger teachers (Tables 9 and 11). Student architecture: We compare student architectures such as BiLSTM and Transformer with respect to configuration and performance (Figure 3, Table 15 in Appendix), and observe that BiLSTM performs better under low-latency configurations, whereas the Transformer surpasses it with greater depth and a higher latency budget. Unlabeled transfer data: We examined the data dimension in Tables 3 and 6 and found unlabeled data to be crucial for transferring knowledge from pretrained teachers to shallow students and narrowing the performance gap. We observed that a moderate amount of unlabeled transfer samples (0.7–1.5 MM) produces the best student, while larger transfer sets do not yield substantial gains. This is especially beneficial for low-resource NER, with only 100 labeled samples per language as in Table 6.
source_text: Teacher hidden representation and distillation schedule: Internal teacher representations help in distillation, although a naive combination hurts the student model. We show that a distillation schedule with stagewise optimization, gradual unfreezing with a cosine learning rate scheduler (D4.1 + D4.2 in Table 3 ) obtains the best performance. We also show that the middle layers of the teacher are easier to distil by shallow students and result in the best performance (Table 8) . Additionally, the student performance improves with bigger and better teachers (Tables 9 and 11) Student architecture: We compare different student architectures like BiLSTM and Transformer in terms of configuration and performance ( Figure 3 , Table 15 in Appendix), and observe BiLSTM to perform better at low-latency configurations, whereas the Transformer outperforms the former with more depth and higher latency budget. Unlabeled transfer data: We explored data dimension in Tables 3 and 6 and observed unlabeled data to be the key for knowledge transfer from pretrained teachers to shallow students and bridge the performance gap. We observed a moderate amount of unlabeled transfer samples (0.7-1.5 MM) lead to the best student, whereas larger amounts of transfer data does not result in significant gains. This is particularly helpful for low-resource NER (with only 100 labeled samples per language as in Table 6 ).

text: Our paper investigates the problem of learning to detect and annotate questions in telephone calls to emergency medical services in English. This task is challenging in two main respects: 1. Noise: a typical emergency medical service phone call differs substantially from the data used in most standard speech corpora. In particular, emergency calls are inherently noisy because highly stressful conversations are transmitted over poor-quality telephone lines. Automatic speech recognition (ASR) and subsequent text-based processing rapidly become impractical in such noisy settings, where word error rates (WER) are considerably higher than those observed on standard benchmark datasets (Han et al., 2017). For this reason, we introduce a sequence labeler that leverages two modalities of a phone call: the audio signal and its transcription into text obtained via an ASR model. In this way, we construct a multimodal Figure 1 shows a speech sequence from our phone call dataset. Two audio segments are highlighted: a question (in blue) and a reported symptom (in yellow).
source_text: Our paper addresses the challenge of learning to discover and label questions in telephone calls to emergency medical services in English. The task is demanding in two key aspects: 1. Noise: A typical phone call to an emergency medical service differs significantly from data within most standard speech datasets. Most importantly, emergency calls are noisy by nature due to very stressful conversations conveyed over poor telephone lines. Automatic speech recognition (ASR) and subsequent text processing quickly becomes prohibitive in such noisy environments, where word error rates (WER) are significantly higher than for standard benchmark data (Han et al., 2017) . For this reason, we propose a sequence labeler that makes use of two modalities of a phone call: audio and its transcription into text by utilizing an ASR model. Hereby we create a multimodal Figure 1 : A speech sequence from our phone call dataset. Two audio segments are highlighted: a question (in blue) and a reported symptom (in yellow).

text: In the QC task, we observe comparable results. The training set consists of approximately 5,400 questions. In the coarse-grained setting (Figure 2b), 6 classes are considered; in the fine-grained setting (Figure 2c), the number of classes is 50. In both cases, BERT diverges when only 1% of the labeled questions are used, that is, roughly 50 questions. It begins to recover when about 20% of the data are employed in the coarse-grained setting, corresponding to approximately 1,000 labeled instances. In the fine-grained setting, our approach performs better up to 50% of the labeled examples. This suggests that, when a larger number of categories is involved, that is, when the classification task is more complex, the semi-supervised setting becomes even more advantageous.
source_text: In the QC task we observe similar outcomes. The training dataset is made of about 5, 400 question. In the coarse-grained setting (figure 2b) 6 classes are involved; in the fine-grained scenario (figure 2c) the number of classes is 50. In both cases, BERT diverges when only 1% of labeled questions are used, i.e., about 50 questions. It starts to com-pensate when using about 20% of the data in the coarse setting (about 1, 000 labeled examples). In the fine-grained scenario, our approach is performing better until 50% of the labeled examples. It seems that, when a large number of categories is involved, i.e., the classification task is more complex, the semi-supervised setting is even more beneficial.

text: Train it when you need it. Prior methods (Hu et al., 2017) learn the joint conditional space by taking all conditions into account simultaneously. However, after training, a new condition cannot be introduced without retraining the entire model. In contrast to these approaches, PPVAE is fully flexible and supports the addition of new conditions. As shown in Figure 1(b), once a condition is introduced, we only need to train a PLUG-INVAE tailored to that condition using Equation 3 (or Equation 5, if samples from other conditions are available). Because PLUGINVAE is text-independent and learns only to map between two latent spaces, the number of parameters trained is only 0.34% (see Section 6.3) of fine-tuning PRE-TRAINVAE or retraining other models. Moreover, although we need to train k PLUGINVAE models for k conditions, the total number of trained parameters remains far smaller than that of existing methods (unless k > 1/0.34% ≈ 294, which is impossible in practice). In addition, we can easily parallelize conditional training to accelerate the process.
source_text: Train it when you need it. Previous methods Hu et al., 2017) learn the joint conditional space by jointly considering all conditions. However, once the model is trained, it is not possible to add a new condition without a full retraining. Different from those approaches, PPVAE is totally flexible that allows adding new conditions. Shown in Figure 1(b) , once a condition is added, we only need to train a PLUG-INVAE specifically for this condition with Equation 3 (or Equation 5, if provided with samples of other conditions). Since PLUGINVAE is textirrelevant and only learns to map between two latent spaces, the training number of parameters is only 0.34% (see Section 6.3) of fine-tuning PRE-TRAINVAE or retraining other models. Additionally, although we need to train k PLUGINVAE for k conditions, the total number of trained parameters is still much smaller than existing methods (unless k > 1/0.34% ≈ 294, which is impossible in actual applications). Plus, we can parallel the conditional training to speed up the process easily.

text: We conduct our evaluations with the AWD-LSTM (Merity et al., 2017) and Mixture of Softmaxes (MoS) (Yang et al., 2017) language models. Both models are trained on the Wikitext-2 corpus (Merity et al., 2016) with default hyperparameters, except that the dimensionality is fixed to d = {50, 100, 200}. The AWD-LSTM model is trained for 500 epochs, whereas the MoS model is trained for 200 epochs, yielding the perplexities reported in Table 1.

text: Multimodal language analysis is a recent research direction in natural language processing (Zadeh et al., 2018b) that seeks to characterize language through the text, vision, and acoustic modalities. These studies have mainly addressed sentiment analysis, emotion recognition (Zadeh et al., 2018d), and personality trait recognition (Park et al., 2014). Research in this area often emphasizes novel multimodal neural architectures (Pham et al., 2019; Hazarika et al., 2018) as well as multimodal fusion strategies (Tsai et al., 2018). In relation to the material discussed in this paper, we review several models in this domain, including TFN, MARN, MFN, RMFN, and MulT. Tensor Fusion Network (TFN) constructs a multi-dimensional tensor to explicitly model all possible interactions among the three modalities: unimodal, bimodal, and trimodal. Multiattention Recurrent Network (MARN) (Zadeh et al., 2018c) employs three distinct hybrid LSTM memories that can propagate cross-modal interactions. Memory Fusion Network (Zadeh et al., 2018a) aligns the information from three separate LSTMs via a multi-view gated memory. Recurrent Memory Fusion Network (RMFN) models the fine-grained interactions among modalities in a multi-stage fashion, allowing each stage to attend to a subset of signals. Multimodal Transformer for Unaligned Multimodal Language Sequences (MulT) (Tsai et al., 2019) uses three Transformers, one for each modality, to capture interactions with the other two modalities in a self-attentive manner. The outputs of the three Transformers are combined through late fusion.
source_text: Multimodal language analyses is a recent research trend in natural language processing (Zadeh et al., 2018b ) that helps us understand language from the modalities of text, vision and acoustic. These analyses have particularly focused on the tasks of sentiment analysis , emotion recognition (Zadeh et al., 2018d) , and personality traits recognition (Park et al., 2014) . Works in this area often focus on novel multimodal neural architectures (Pham et al., 2019; Hazarika et al., 2018) and multimodal fusion approaches Tsai et al., 2018) . Related to content in this paper, we discuss some of the models in this domain including TFN, MARN, MFN, RMFN and MulT. Tensor Fusion Network (TFN) creates a multi-dimensional tensor to explicitly capture all possible interactions between the three modalities: unimodal, bimodal and trimodal. Multiattention Recurrent Network (MARN) (Zadeh et al., 2018c) uses three separate hybrid LSTM memories that have the ability to propagate the cross-modal interactions. Memory Fusion Network (Zadeh et al., 2018a) synchronizes the information from three separate LSTMs through a multi-view gated memory. Recurrent Memory Fusion Network (RMFN) captures the nuanced interactions among the modalities in a multi-stage manner, giving each stage the ability to focus on a subset of signals. Multimodal Transformer for Unaligned Multimodal Language Sequences (MulT) (Tsai et al., 2019) deploys three Transformers -each for one modality -to capture the interactions with the other two modalities in a selfattentive manner. The information from the three Transformers are aggregated through late-fusion.

text: Finally, research on multimodal or multi-view deep learning (Ngiam et al., 2011; Li et al., 2018) provides useful insights for integrating multiple data modalities or views for the same learning task. Nevertheless, most of this literature does not apply directly to our setting: i) the audio-text modality is severely underrepresented, ii) the models are usually not designed to operate online, and iii) most tasks are formulated as document-level classification rather than sequence labeling (Zadeh et al., 2018).
source_text: Finally, research into multimodal or multi-view deep learning (Ngiam et al., 2011; Li et al., 2018) offers insights to effectively combine multiple data modalities or views on the same learning problem. However, most work does not directly apply to our problem: i) the audio-text modality is significantly under-represented, ii) the models are typically not required to work online, and iii) most tasks are cast as document-level classification and not sequence labeling (Zadeh et al., 2018) .

text: Two explanations may account for this pattern: (1) the models have acquired a human-like LOW bias, or (2) the models exhibit a recency bias that preferentially attaches to the lower nominal. In English, these two hypotheses make overlapping predictions. The second account is perhaps somewhat weakened by the findings reported in Section 4, where both attachment types were learnable despite any recency bias. Nevertheless, we know that other syntactic attachment biases can affect RC attachment in human processing (Scheepers, 2003). It is possible that other attachment relations, such as prepositional phrase attachment, differ in the proportions of attachment biases present in the training data. Conflicting attachment biases across multiple constructions may therefore compel the model to fall back on a “default” recency bias in ambiguous cases.
source_text: There are two possible reasons for this patterning: (1) the models have learned a human-like LOW bias, or (2) the models have a recency bias that favors attachment to the lower nominal. These two hypotheses have overlapping predictions in English. The second hypothesis is perhaps weakened by the results of Section 4, where both attachment types were learnable despite any recency bias. However, we know that other syntactic attachment biases can influence RC attachment in humans (Scheepers, 2003) . It could be that other kinds of attachment (such as prepositional phrase attachment) have varying proportions of attachment biases in the training data. Perhaps conflicting attachment biases across multiple constructions force the model to resort to the use of a 'default' recency bias in cases of ambiguity.

text: For dependency parsing and POS tagging, the most notable contribution not specific to English is the CoNLL 2018 Shared Task (Zeman et al., 2018), in which first place in the LAS ranking was awarded to the HIT-SCIR team (Che et al., 2018). They employed ’s Deep Biaffine parser and its extension described in , together with deep contextualized ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018), while capping the training set at 20 million words. First place in universal POS tagging was awarded to Smith et al. (2018), who used two separate instances of ’s tagger.

text: Most studies on flat text classification primarily concentrate on introducing a range of novel neural architectures (Conneau et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2015). Other methods incorporate a transfer learning stage in order to exploit unlabeled data. McCann et al. (2017) employed the encoder component of a neural machine translation model to supply contextual information to other natural language processing models, whereas Howard and Ruder (2018) pre-trained a language model on a general-domain monolingual corpus and subsequently fine-tuned it for text classification tasks.
source_text: Most of the studies for flat text classification primarily focus on proposing a variety of novel neural architectures (Conneau et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2015) . Other approaches involve a transfer learning step to take advantage of unlabelled data. McCann et al. (2017) used the encoder unit of a neural machine translation model to provide context for other natural language processing models, while Howard and Ruder (2018) pre-trained a language model on a general-domain monolingual corpus and then fine-tuned it for text classification tasks.
